Client: Um, net it’s like… maybe eight thousand a month?
Advisor: Okay—so that’s take-home. When I say gross, I mean before taxes and deductions. Do you know the annual gross number, even roughly?
Client: Right, sorry. Gross is about one hundred three thousand a year—$103,350, give or take.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. And that’s just you—since it’s only you in the household, right?
Client: Yes, just me as the adult.

Client: That’s basically nothing. I think it was an old entry—there isn’t a second pension paying.
Advisor: Totally fine. Switching to expenses—what do you spend in a typical month, all-in? Mortgage or rent, utilities, food, insurance, the usual.
Client: About three thousand a month. Some months a little higher, but $3,000 is the baseline.
Advisor: Okay. And debt payments—credit cards, car loan, student loans—anything like that with a monthly payment?
Client: No, I don’t have any debt payments right now.

Client: Um, net it’s like… maybe eight thousand a month?
Advisor: Okay—so that’s take-home. When I say gross, I mean before taxes and deductions. Do you know the annual gross number, even roughly?
Client: Right, sorry. Gross is about one hundred three thousand a year—$103,350, give or take.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. And that’s just you—since it’s only you in the household, right?
Client: Yes, just me as the adult.

Client: No, nothing active right now.
Advisor: Alright. I’m going to circle back to something you said at the start—stability month to month. With about $3,000 in monthly expenses and no debt payments, that helps. But I want to confirm your investable assets total—if you add up the taxable, retirement, bank/cash, and private markets, what do you consider your investable total?
Client: I think it’s around six ninety-ish? Like $690,000.
Advisor: Hang on—let me check I heard you right. Is it closer to $692,550 as the total investable assets number you’re using?
Client: Yes—$692,550. Sorry, I rounded it in my head.

Client: Insurance, I believe.
Advisor: Okay. And then your home—your primary residence—what do you think it’s worth today?
Client: About $440,600.
Advisor: And that home value—do you track it on the same advisor platform too?
Client: Yes, that’s where I saw it listed.

Advisor: Hi Linda—good to meet you. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, how are you holding up today?
Client: Hi Alex. I’m okay—busy, honestly. It’s been a lot since my husband passed, but I’m managing.
Advisor: I hear you. We’ll keep this practical. Tell me a little about you and what you want this plan to do for you right now.
Client: Mostly I want stability month to month, and I don’t want to make some irreversible mistake. I’m still working, but I’m thinking about when I can stop.
Advisor: Got it. Going back to that monthly stability piece—let’s start with income. Roughly what’s your gross household income in a year right now, before taxes?

Client: Um, net it’s like… maybe eight thousand a month?
Advisor: Okay—so that’s take-home. When I say gross, I mean before taxes and deductions. Do you know the annual gross number, even roughly?
Client: Right, sorry. Gross is about one hundred three thousand a year—$103,350, give or take.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. And that’s just you—since it’s only you in the household, right?
Client: Yes, just me as the adult.

Advisor: Totally get it. So one adult in the household—just you.
Client: Yes, one adult.
Advisor: Great. Next one is more of a “how do you want the money to behave” question. Earlier you sounded pretty growth-leaning, but cautious when it comes to stability for the kids.
Advisor: If you had to pick a label—conservative, moderate, or growth—where do you land right now?
Client: Growth. I mean, not reckless, but growth.

Advisor: Got it—growth, but pragmatic. When you say “not reckless,” do you mean you’d still tolerate some market swings as long as there’s a plan?
Client: Yes. I don’t like surprises, but I can handle volatility if I know why we’re doing it.
Advisor: That makes sense. Quick admin thing: are we working with you in the U.S. RIA channel—like a U.S.-based registered investment advisor relationship—or is this through something else?
Client: Wait—RIA like… retirement income account?
Advisor: Easy mix-up. Here RIA just means “registered investment advisor,” basically the U.S. advisory setup—not an account type.

Client: I think it’s around eighteen thousand two hundred a year.
Advisor: And the interest and dividends—what does that run annually, roughly?
Client: That one’s bigger—about forty-two and a half thousand a year. $42,550 is what I remember.
Advisor: Okay. Rental income—do you think of that as monthly rent, or you just track it annually?
Client: Annually. It’s about thirty-one thousand a year.

Advisor: Perfect, thank you. And just to confirm household size—how many adults are in the household?
Client: Uh, two—me and my husband.
Advisor: Okay—hang on, Linda, I thought you’d mentioned you’re widowed. Did I misunderstand, or are you saying another adult lives with you?
Client: Oh—sorry, no, you’re right. It’s just me. One adult. I was thinking of… I don’t know, old habit.
Advisor: Totally get it. So one adult in the household—just you.

Advisor: Hi Linda—good to meet you. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, how are you holding up today?
Client: Hi Alex. I’m okay—busy, honestly. It’s been a lot since my husband passed, but I’m managing.
Advisor: I hear you. We’ll keep this practical. Tell me a little about you and what you want this plan to do for you right now.
Client: Mostly I want stability month to month, and I don’t want to make some irreversible mistake. I’m still working, but I’m thinking about when I can stop.

Advisor: Great. And earlier you confirmed California—so U.S., California. Okay.
Client: Yep.
Advisor: Now taxes—this is the one people hate. When we talk about your tax bracket, do you know roughly where you land? Like 24%, 32%, 37%—something like that.
Client: I think it’s 35%? Somewhere in there.
Advisor: Close—so are you thinking federal marginal bracket? The top rate you hit on the next dollar?

Client: Yeah, seriously. Anyway—go ahead.
Advisor: Alright—going back to that income mix you mentioned earlier, I want to make sure I’ve got your work piece right, Linda. Are you still working currently?
Client: Yes, I’m still employed.
Advisor: Got it. And when you think about your job, what bucket would you put it in—like operations, finance, HR, that kind of thing?
Client: Operations, yeah.

Advisor: Alright—going back to that income mix you mentioned earlier, I want to make sure I’ve got your work piece right, Linda. Are you still working currently?
Client: Yes, I’m still employed.
Advisor: Got it. And when you think about your job, what bucket would you put it in—like operations, finance, HR, that kind of thing?
Client: Operations, yeah.
Advisor: Okay. And just to pin down the pay number—what’s your gross annual from the job, roughly, before taxes and anything coming out of the paycheck?

Client: Right, it’s just me, so it should be straightforward.
Advisor: Totally. Okay—switching gears slightly—can I grab your date of birth, Linda? Month, day, year.
Client: Uh, August 16th, 1961.
Advisor: August 16, 1961—thank you.
Client: Yeah.

Advisor: Also through an insurance provider, or something else?
Client: Insurance, I believe.
Advisor: Okay. And then your home—your primary residence—what do you think it’s worth today?
Client: About $440,600.
Advisor: And that home value—do you track it on the same advisor platform too?

Advisor: Okay—so that’s take-home. When I say gross, I mean before taxes and deductions. Do you know the annual gross number, even roughly?
Client: Right, sorry. Gross is about one hundred three thousand a year—$103,350, give or take.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. And that’s just you—since it’s only you in the household, right?

Client: I was already in, thankfully. It was a mess out there.
Advisor: Yeah, same. Okay—back to it.
Advisor: I’m going to ask one slightly awkward labeling question, Linda. In my notes I have you as “Client 1.” Is that how we should label you, or should you be “Client 2” for any reason?
Client: I’m Client 1.
Advisor: Okay—hang on—so you’re saying Client 1.

Advisor: Zero—thank you. And that pension line—just confirming—it's yours, Linda, not anyone else’s income, right?
Client: Yes, it’s mine.
Advisor: Perfect. So we’ll keep it as a pension income line, owned by you, annual frequency, but currently $0 coming in. That sound right?
Client: Yes.
Advisor: Quick pause—when you said “annual,” you meant it pays once a year when it’s active, not that it’s an annual total of a monthly payment, right?

Client: Uh… like ten grand a year? Maybe?
Advisor: Okay—about $10k-ish. Let me sanity-check that with you, because earlier we were separating “frequency” from “annual total.” When you say monthly, do you mean you get a smaller amount each month, and over the year it totals around eleven-and-a-half thousand or so?
Client: Yeah—sorry, that’s more accurate. It’s about $11,650 for the year when I add it up.
Advisor: No apology needed—thank you. So: bonus income, paid monthly, about $11,650 annualized, and it’s yours, Linda.
Client: Yes.

Client: I was already in, thankfully. It was a mess out there.
Advisor: Yeah, same. Okay—back to it.
Advisor: I’m going to ask one slightly awkward labeling question, Linda. In my notes I have you as “Client 1.” Is that how we should label you, or should you be “Client 2” for any reason?
Client: I’m Client 1.
Advisor: Okay—hang on—so you’re saying Client 1.

Advisor: Perfect, thank you. And that’s just you—since it’s only you in the household, right?
Client: Yes, just me as the adult.
Advisor: One sec—let me note that down. Now, I know you mentioned multiple income sources earlier when you filled out the basics. Can we sanity-check those quickly—pension, interest/dividends, rental, and a bonus?
Client: Yeah. I have a pension that comes monthly, and then investment income monthly, and rental income once a year, and a bonus that’s kind of monthly-ish.
Advisor: On the pension—about how much is that per year if you had to ballpark it?

Advisor: I hear you. We’ll keep this practical. Tell me a little about you and what you want this plan to do for you right now.

Client: Um, net it’s like… maybe eight thousand a month?
Advisor: Okay—so that’s take-home. When I say gross, I mean before taxes and deductions. Do you know the annual gross number, even roughly?

Advisor: And just so I don’t repeat the earlier mistake—this is not that zero pension line we talked about. This is separate and it’s real cash flow each month.
Client: Correct.
Advisor: Okay. While we’re still in “income,” I’m going to quickly name the other ones we already covered so I don’t lose the thread: pension about $18,200 a year monthly, interest and dividends about $42,550 a year monthly, rental about $31,000 a year annual, and then this bonus about $11,650 a year monthly.
Client: Yes, that matches what I remember.
Advisor: Great. Now, tiny pause—when you said “monthly,” you meant it comes in every month, not that it’s a monthly amount of $11,650, right?

Advisor: Great. And I saw a second pension line that looked like zero—do you have another pension that just isn’t started yet, or is that basically nothing?
Client: That’s basically nothing. I think it was an old entry—there isn’t a second pension paying.
Advisor: Totally fine. Switching to expenses—what do you spend in a typical month, all-in? Mortgage or rent, utilities, food, insurance, the usual.
Client: About three thousand a month. Some months a little higher, but $3,000 is the baseline.
Advisor: Okay. And debt payments—credit cards, car loan, student loans—anything like that with a monthly payment?

Client: I was already in, thankfully. It was a mess out there.
Advisor: Yeah, same. Okay—back to it.
Advisor: I’m going to ask one slightly awkward labeling question, Linda. In my notes I have you as “Client 1.” Is that how we should label you, or should you be “Client 2” for any reason?
Client: I’m Client 1.
Advisor: Okay—hang on—so you’re saying Client 1.

Client: Sure. It’s small, but yeah, let’s do it.
Advisor: When you say “private markets,” are we talking like private equity, a REIT, hedge fund—what bucket would you put it in?
Client: I’d call it alternatives. It’s not stocks and bonds, it’s like an alternative sleeve.
Advisor: Alternatives—perfect. And just to confirm, that alternatives holding is in your name only, Linda, right?
Client: Yes, it’s mine.

Advisor: Just to be super clear—zero dollars a month toward debt service?
Client: Correct. Zero.
Advisor: Good. Now assets—this is where it can get a little messy, so stop me if I’m mixing things up. You’ve got a few buckets: a taxable account, a retirement account like a 401(k) or IRA, some bank cash, and a small private markets piece. Does that sound right?
Client: Yes, that’s right.
Advisor: Let’s go one by one. The taxable account—about what’s the value there right now?
